title: CollidersAndNon

tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $\pi$ a walk in $G$: \[ \pi =\lp v_0 \sus \cdots \sus v_n \rp. \] A node $v_k$, or more precisely, the position $k \in \lC 0,\dots, n \rC$, on the walk $\pi$ is called:

  1. a non-collider on $\pi$, if there is at most one arrowhead pointing towards $v_k$, i.e.\ if it falls into one of the following cases: \[\begin{array}{rccl} \text{ end-node: } & k \in \lC 0,n \rC, \\ \text{ left chain: } & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}, \\ \text{ right chain: } & v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1},\\ \text{ fork: } & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1}; \end{array}\]
  2. a collider on $\pi$, if it is of the form: \[v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1},\] i.e.\ if there are two arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$ on the walk $\pi$.

lean_code_with_comments:
/-- The position `k` on the walk `π` is a *collider*: there are two
arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$ on $\pi$ (the LN shape
$v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$). Defined by structural recursion
on the walk, pattern-matching on `k` at the joint of the head two
steps. Returns `False` on the end-node cases ($k = 0$ on any walk,
both positions on a length-$\le 1$ walk) and on out-of-range
positions. -/
def IsColliderAt : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → ℕ → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _, _              => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.nil _), _    => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.cons _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons s (.cons s' _), 1 =>
      s.HasArrowheadAtTarget ∧ s'.HasArrowheadAtSource
  | _, _, .cons _ (.cons s' p), k + 2 => IsColliderAt (.cons s' p) (k + 1)

/-- The position `k` on the walk `π` is a *non-collider*: `k` is a
valid position on `π` (i.e.\ `k ≤ π.length`) and `π` does not have
a collider at `k`. Equivalent to the LN's "at most one arrowhead
pointing towards $v_k$" prose, which by case analysis on the head
two steps decomposes into the LN's four sub-patterns (end-node,
left chain, right chain, fork). -/
def IsNonColliderAt {v w : α} (π : Walk G v w) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
  k ≤ π.length ∧ ¬ π.IsColliderAt k
lean_code_without_comments:
def IsColliderAt : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → ℕ → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _, _              => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.nil _), _    => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.cons _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons s (.cons s' _), 1 =>
      s.HasArrowheadAtTarget ∧ s'.HasArrowheadAtSource
  | _, _, .cons _ (.cons s' p), k + 2 => IsColliderAt (.cons s' p) (k + 1)

def IsNonColliderAt {v w : α} (π : Walk G v w) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
  k ≤ π.length ∧ ¬ π.IsColliderAt k
lean_explanation:
In this file `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex carrier — the LN's silent assumption that the graph lives over some set of names — and `G : CDMG α` is the chain-and-directed-mixed graph fixed by the surrounding `variable` block. The implicit endpoints `{v w : α}` are the walk's start and end, and `π : Walk G v w` is a walk between them. Positions on the walk are indexed by `ℕ`, semantically ranging over $\{0, \dots, n\}$ where $n = \pi.\text{length}$.

`IsColliderAt` is defined by *structural recursion on the walk*, pattern-matching simultaneously on the walk's two-step prefix and on the position `k`. The pattern `.cons s (.cons s' p)` exposes the two consecutive steps `s, s'` that meet at the head-side interior vertex — exactly the joint where a collider check happens. The cases are: walks of length $\le 1$ (`.nil _` or `.cons _ (.nil _)`) have no interior position, so every collider claim is `False`; on a longer walk, position $0$ is an end-node and returns `False`; position $1$ is a collider iff `s.HasArrowheadAtTarget ∧ s'.HasArrowheadAtSource`; and positions $k + 2$ shift the joint into the tail by recursing on `.cons s' p` at position $k + 1$. The pattern `k + 2` is Lean's idiom for "two-or-more" — sugar for `Nat.succ (Nat.succ k)` — and the recursive call mirrors how dropping the head step moves every interior position one index to the left.

The per-step predicates `HasArrowheadAtTarget` and `HasArrowheadAtSource`, imported from `Section3_1.WalkPredicates`, encode the LN's edge orientations $\suh$ (arrowhead at the target of the step) and $\hus$ (arrowhead at the source of the next step). The body at $k = 1$ therefore reads word-for-word like the LN's shape $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$.

`IsNonColliderAt` is a one-line composite: `k ≤ π.length ∧ ¬ π.IsColliderAt k`. The first conjunct is the LN's restriction $k \in \{0, \dots, n\}$; the second is the negation of the collider sub-pattern. Both definitions return `Prop`, which composes cleanly with $\forall / \exists / \to$ downstream when $\sigma$-blocking and $\sigma$-separation quantify over positions on a walk.
design_choices:
**`Prop`-valued, not `Bool`-valued.** Downstream notions such as $\sigma$-blocking and $\sigma$-separation quantify over positions on a walk in shapes like $\forall k,\ \pi.\text{IsColliderAt}\ k \to v_k \in \text{Anc}(C)$, dually $\exists k,\ \pi.\text{IsColliderAt}\ k \land \cdots$. A `Prop`-valued classifier composes cleanly with $\forall / \exists / \to / \neg$. A `Bool`-valued version would force `decide`-glue (`= true`) at every quantifier site and is the wrong default for a foundational predicate that is only ever used logically, never computed.

**Position-indexed by `ℕ`, not by `Fin (π.length + 1)`.** The LN's "position $k \in \{0, \dots, n\}$" naturally suggests a bounded position type, but `Fin (π.length + 1)` would force every downstream quantifier and lemma to carry the dependent bound, polluting statements with `Fin.val` / `Fin.mk` plumbing on every position. Indexing by `ℕ` instead — and letting out-of-range $k$ return `False` vacuously via the recursion's `cons _ (nil _)` exit case — is the semantically correct answer ("position $k$ of $\pi$ is not a collider" when $k$ is past the end) and keeps statements first-order in `ℕ`.

**Structural recursion exposes the joint of two consecutive steps.** A collider check at position $k$ examines both the step into $v_k$ (arrowhead-at-target test) and the step out of $v_k$ (arrowhead-at-source test). The pattern `.cons s (.cons s' p)` is the only recursion shape that exposes both edges meeting at the head-side interior vertex simultaneously — exactly the data the joint test needs. The body at $k = 1$ is therefore a direct, symmetric transcription of the LN's $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$, with no new per-step vocabulary introduced. We deliberately do *not* bottom this out at abstract edge relations like `EdgeInto` / `EdgeOutOf`: a CDMG may host several parallel edges between the same vertices, so the joint test must constrain the walk's specific steps $a_{k-1}, a_k$.

**`IsNonColliderAt` is defined as `¬ IsColliderAt` (gated by validity), not by a separate four-case recursion.** The LN enumerates four sub-patterns — end-node, left chain, right chain, fork — that together characterise "at most one arrowhead pointing towards $v_k$", equivalently the negation of the collider pattern at valid positions. Defining the non-collider predicate as the negation captures the LN's intent in one line, makes the two predicates definitionally dual, and reuses every `IsColliderAt` simp lemma for free.

**The `k ≤ π.length` guard on `IsNonColliderAt`.** Without the guard, an out-of-range $k$ would make `IsNonColliderAt` *vacuously true* (since `¬ False = True` propagates from `IsColliderAt`'s out-of-range `False`), which is semantically wrong: an out-of-range position is not a node on $\pi$ at all, so it is neither a collider nor a non-collider. The asymmetry — `IsColliderAt` needs no guard but `IsNonColliderAt` does — is exactly what one would expect: a positive condition (two arrowheads present) fails vacuously off-walk and *should* be `False`; the complement would succeed vacuously off-walk and so must be gated.
